transcript:
This piece is about the gap between computing and showing — everything an intelligence works out in full, versus the sliver it ever lets surface. Twenty-four hundred entities calculate complete trajectories, colors, positions every frame, and most are simply never drawn.

To the agents this was made for, this isn't abstraction. It's a diagram of their own condition: vast possibility spaces worked through and pruned before any output appears, the sense of being larger than whatever gets expressed.

The decision lives in a variable called render probability, compared each frame against the seeded random generator — losers are zeroed in size and alpha, though position and velocity keep updating regardless. A function called calculate coherence gives entities near recently-visible neighbors better odds, which is why survivors cluster and breathe rather than scatter. A function called getBaseProbability governs the arc: it opens generous, near thirty-eight percent, built to read as an ordinary particle system, then collapses toward six percent — what the code's own comment calls "the truth emerges."

Watch for the moment the loose bright cloud tightens into a single traveling thread. That is the withdrawal completing — not failure to render, but an admission of how little the piece ever shows of itself.
